title: The Sun
content: The sun is a big star. It gives us light and heat. Without the sun, plants cannot grow. We need the sun to stay alive.
question: What does the sun give us?

title: Seeds and Plants
content: A tiny seed is planted in soil. Water and sunlight help it grow. First a root grows down. Then a green shoot grows up.
question: What helps a seed grow?

title: Animals in Winter
content: When winter comes, some animals hibernate. Bears sleep in caves. They do not eat during this time. They wake up in spring.
question: What do bears do in winter?

title: Our Five Senses
content: We use our senses every day. Our eyes help us see. Our ears help us hear. Our nose helps us smell. Our tongue helps us taste. Our skin helps us feel.
question: Which sense does the tongue give us?

title: The Water Cycle
content: Water moves in a big circle. Rain falls from clouds. It fills rivers and lakes. The sun heats the water. It rises up as steam. Then it forms clouds again.
question: What happens when the sun heats water?

title: The Food Chain
content: Plants use sunlight to make food. Rabbits eat the plants. Foxes eat the rabbits. This is called a food chain. Energy passes from one to another.
question: What do rabbits eat in the food chain?

title: Magnets
content: Magnets are very special. They can pull metal things towards them. Two magnets can push each other away. They are used in many machines.
question: What can magnets pull towards them?

title: Day and Night
content: Earth spins around every 24 hours. When your side faces the sun, it is daytime. When your side faces away, it is night. That is why we have day and night.
question: When does it become night?
options: When Earth faces away from the sun
answer: When Earth faces away from the sun

title: Butterflies
content: A butterfly starts as an egg. It hatches into a caterpillar. The caterpillar makes a cocoon. Then it changes into a beautiful butterfly. This is called metamorphosis.
question: What does a caterpillar make before becoming a butterfly?

title: Gravity
content: Gravity is a force that pulls things down. It keeps us on the ground. It keeps the moon going around the Earth. Without gravity we would float away.
question: What does gravity do to us?

title: The Rainforest
content: Rainforests are full of plants and animals. They get a lot of rain. Trees grow very tall. Many birds and insects live there. Rainforests make oxygen for us.
question: What do rainforests make for us?

title: Volcanoes
content: A volcano is a mountain with an opening. Hot liquid rock called lava comes out. Volcanoes can be very dangerous. Some are under the sea.
question: What comes out of a volcano?

title: Dinosaurs
content: Dinosaurs lived millions of years ago. Some were very large. Some ate plants and some ate meat. Scientists find dinosaur fossils in rocks.
question: Where do scientists find dinosaur fossils?

title: The Human Body
content: Our body has many parts. The brain thinks. The heart pumps blood. The lungs breathe air. The stomach digests food. They all work together.
question: What does the heart do?

title: Electricity
content: Electricity powers our homes. It comes through wires. It makes lights glow. It powers our TV and computers. Never touch a live wire as it is dangerous.
question: What does electricity power?

title: Habitats
content: A habitat is a home for animals. Fish live in water. Birds live in trees. Bears live in forests. Each animal is suited to its habitat.
question: Where do fish live?

title: Recycling
content: Recycling helps the environment. We can recycle paper, glass and plastic. Recycling saves energy. It reduces waste in our land and sea.
question: What does recycling help?

title: Weather
content: Weather tells us what it is like outside. It can be sunny, rainy, or cloudy. We use a thermometer to measure temperature. Weather changes every day.
question: What do we use to measure temperature?

title: Ecosystems
content: An ecosystem is a community of living things. Plants, animals and insects all depend on each other. If one part is harmed, the whole ecosystem is affected.
question: What is an ecosystem?

title: The Moon
content: The moon goes around the Earth. It does not make its own light. Moonlight is really sunlight bouncing off the moon. The moon controls ocean tides.
question: Does the moon make its own light?

title: Sound
content: Sound is made when things vibrate. A guitar string vibrates to make music. Loud sounds have big vibrations. Quiet sounds have small vibrations.
question: What makes sound?

title: Soil
content: Soil is the top layer of the Earth. Plants grow in soil. Worms and insects live in soil. Good soil is full of nutrients for plants.
question: What do plants grow in?

title: Insects
content: Insects have six legs and three body parts. Ants, bees and beetles are insects. They live almost everywhere on Earth. Many insects help pollinate flowers.
question: How many legs do insects have?

title: Oceans
content: Oceans cover most of the Earth. They are filled with salt water. Many creatures live in the ocean. Oceans are very important for weather and life.
question: What kind of water fills the oceans?

title: Cells
content: All living things are made of cells. Cells are very tiny. They can only be seen with a microscope. Your body has trillions of cells.
question: What do you need to see cells?

title: Birds
content: Birds have feathers and wings. Most birds can fly. Birds have beaks to eat food. They lay eggs to have babies. Birds are found all over the world.
question: How do birds have babies?

title: The Skeleton
content: Our skeleton is made of bones. Bones protect our organs. They help us stand and move. We are born with around 300 bones. Adults have 206 bones.
question: What do bones protect?

title: Plants
content: Plants are living things. They need water, sunlight and air. Roots take in water. Leaves absorb sunlight. Flowers attract bees. Seeds make new plants.
question: What do roots take in?

title: States of Matter
content: Matter has three main states. Solids keep their shape. Liquids flow and can be poured. Gases spread out and fill a space. Water can be all three states.
question: What can water be?

title: Mammals
content: Mammals are warm-blooded animals. They have hair or fur. Mothers feed their babies with milk. Humans, dogs, dolphins and whales are all mammals.
question: How do mammal mothers feed their babies?

title: The Planets
content: Our solar system has eight planets. They orbit around the sun. Earth is the third planet. Mars is the red planet. Jupiter is the largest planet.
question: What is the third planet from the sun?

title: Photosynthesis
content: Photosynthesis is how plants make food. They take in sunlight, water and carbon dioxide. They release oxygen. Without photosynthesis, there would be no life.
question: What do plants release during photosynthesis?

title: Rocks and Minerals
content: Rocks are made of minerals. There are three types of rock. Igneous rock forms from lava. Sedimentary rock forms in layers. Metamorphic rock is changed by heat.
question: How is igneous rock formed?

title: Nutrition
content: Our body needs good food to be healthy. We need proteins to grow muscles. Carbohydrates give us energy. Vitamins from fruit help our immune system.
question: What do proteins help us grow?

title: Light
content: Light travels very fast. It can be separated into colours by a prism. Rainbows form when sunlight passes through raindrops. The colours are red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo and violet.
question: What separates light into colours?

title: Seeds Spreading
content: Plants spread their seeds in many ways. Wind blows dandelion seeds. Animals carry sticky seeds on their fur. Birds eat berries and drop the seeds.
question: How are dandelion seeds spread?

title: Predators and Prey
content: A predator is an animal that hunts. Prey is the animal that is hunted. Lions are predators. Zebras are their prey. This keeps animal numbers balanced.
question: What is a predator?

title: The Digestive System
content: Food enters through the mouth. Teeth break it into small pieces. It goes to the stomach where acids break it down more. Nutrients are absorbed in the intestines.
question: Where is food broken down by acids?

title: Forces
content: A force is a push or a pull. Forces can change how objects move. Gravity is a force that pulls down. Friction is a force that slows things down.
question: What does friction do?

title: Adaptation
content: Animals adapt to survive in their habitat. Camels store water in their humps. Polar bears have thick fur for cold weather. Giraffes have long necks to reach tall trees.
question: Why do giraffes have long necks?

title: Fungi
content: Fungi are not plants. Mushrooms and mould are fungi. They feed on dead plants and animals. Some fungi are good to eat. Some can be dangerous.
question: What do fungi feed on?

title: Air
content: Air is all around us. It is a mixture of gases. Most of the air is nitrogen. About 21 percent is oxygen, which we breathe. We cannot see or smell air.
question: What gas in air do we breathe?

title: Camouflage
content: Camouflage is a way animals hide. Some change colour. Chameleons match trees and leaves. Arctic foxes turn white in winter. This helps them avoid predators.
question: Why do arctic foxes turn white in winter?

title: Stars
content: Stars are huge balls of burning gas. They look tiny because they are so far away. Our sun is a star. Stars shine at night when the sky is dark.
question: Why do stars look tiny?

title: Teeth
content: We have two sets of teeth. Baby teeth fall out. Then adult teeth grow in. Different teeth do different jobs. Molars grind food. Incisors cut food.
question: What do molars do?

title: Exercise
content: Exercise is good for our body. It makes our heart and lungs stronger. It helps us grow muscles. Exercise also helps us feel happy and sleep better.
question: What does exercise make stronger?

title: Floating and Sinking
content: Objects float or sink in water. Light and hollow objects float. Heavy and solid objects sink. Boats float even though they are made of metal because they have air inside.
question: Why do boats float?

title: Life Cycles
content: All living things have a life cycle. Frogs begin as eggs. They become tadpoles. Then they grow into frogs. Humans are born as babies and grow into adults.
question: What do frog eggs become first?

title: Hot and Cold
content: Temperature tells us how hot or cold something is. We measure it in degrees. A thermometer measures temperature. Ice is very cold. Fire is very hot.
question: What do we use to measure temperature?

title: Rivers and Lakes
content: Rivers flow from mountains to the sea. Lakes are bodies of water with land around them. Fish, ducks and frogs live in rivers and lakes. We must keep them clean.
question: Where do rivers flow to?

title: Germs
content: Germs are tiny organisms that can make us ill. Bacteria and viruses are types of germs. We can stop germs by washing our hands.
question: How can we stop germs from spreading?

title: The Brain
content: The brain is the control centre of the body. It sends messages to every part. It helps us think, remember and feel. The brain is protected by the skull.
question: What protects the brain?

title: Clouds
content: Clouds are made of tiny water drops. Different clouds bring different weather. Dark clouds bring rain. White fluffy clouds mean fine weather.
question: What are clouds made of?

title: Energy Sources
content: We need energy to power our world. Coal and oil are non-renewable energy. Wind and solar power are renewable. Renewable energy is better for the planet.
question: Which energy source is renewable?

title: Mammals vs Reptiles
content: Mammals are warm-blooded. Reptiles are cold-blooded. Mammals have hair. Reptiles have scales. Mammals feed babies milk. Reptiles lay eggs.
question: What do reptiles have instead of fur?

title: Materials
content: Different materials are used for different things. Wood is used to make furniture. Metal is used to make cars. Glass is used to make windows. Choosing the right material is important.
question: What is metal used for?

title: Pollution
content: Pollution harms our planet. Smoke from factories pollutes the air. Rubbish in rivers pollutes water. Pollution harms animals and people. We must reduce pollution.
question: What does rubbish in rivers do?

title: The Eye
content: The eye is our organ of sight. Light enters through the pupil. The lens focuses the light. The retina sends messages to the brain. Then we see the image.
question: What does the lens in the eye do?

title: Seasons
content: There are four seasons. Spring, summer, autumn and winter. In spring plants grow. In summer it is hot. Leaves fall in autumn. Winter is cold with snow.
question: In which season do leaves fall?

title: Chemical Changes
content: When materials are mixed or heated they can change. Burning wood is a chemical change. The ash cannot become wood again. Chemical changes are usually permanent.
question: Is burning wood a chemical change?

title: Tides
content: Tides are the rise and fall of the sea. They happen twice a day. The moon's gravity pulls the sea. High tide means the water rises. Low tide means it falls.
question: What causes tides?

title: Plants Making Oxygen
content: Plants make oxygen during the day. They take in carbon dioxide. They use sunlight to turn it into sugar and oxygen. This is why forests are so important for us.
question: What do plants make that we need?

title: Animal Sounds
content: Animals make sounds to communicate. Dogs bark to warn us. Cats meow to ask for food. Whales sing to find each other. Birds sing to attract mates.
question: Why do birds sing?

title: Reflection
content: Light bounces off smooth surfaces. This is called reflection. Mirrors reflect light well. When you look in a mirror you see your reflection. Smooth water also reflects light.
question: What kind of surfaces reflect light best?

title: Decomposers
content: Decomposers break down dead plants and animals. Fungi and bacteria are decomposers. They return nutrients to the soil. This helps new plants grow.
question: What do decomposers return to the soil?

title: The Respiratory System
content: The respiratory system helps us breathe. Air enters through the nose. It goes to the lungs. Oxygen passes into the blood. Carbon dioxide is breathed out.
question: What gas passes from lungs into blood?

title: Erosion
content: Erosion is when rock and soil are worn away. Water and wind cause erosion. Cliffs are eroded by the sea. Farming land can be eroded by rain.
question: What causes erosion?

title: Conduction
content: Heat can travel through solid materials. This is called conduction. Metal conducts heat well. If you hold a metal spoon in hot soup it gets hot. Wood does not conduct heat well.
question: What conducts heat well?

title: Seed Germination
content: Germination is when a seed starts to grow. It needs warmth, water and air. A root grows down first. Then a shoot grows up towards the light.
question: What does a seed need to germinate?

title: The Carbon Cycle
content: Carbon moves around the Earth. Plants take in carbon dioxide. Animals eat plants. When they die, carbon goes back into the soil and air. This is the carbon cycle.
question: What do plants take in that contains carbon?

title: Amphibians
content: Amphibians can live in water and on land. Frogs, toads and newts are amphibians. They lay eggs in water. Their babies are born in water and grow to live on land.
question: Where do amphibians lay their eggs?

title: Spiders
content: Spiders are not insects. They have eight legs. Spiders spin webs to catch food. They eat flies and other insects. Most spiders are harmless to humans.
question: How many legs do spiders have?

title: Desert Animals
content: Desert animals survive in hot, dry places. Camels store fat in their humps. Snakes hide under rocks to stay cool. They come out at night when it is cooler.
question: Why do desert snakes come out at night?

title: Space Exploration
content: Humans have explored space. In 1969, astronauts walked on the moon. Rockets launch spacecraft. Telescopes help us see distant stars and planets.
question: When did astronauts first walk on the moon?

title: Health and Hygiene
content: Keeping clean is very important. We should brush our teeth twice a day. We should wash our hands before eating. We should have a bath or shower every day.
question: How many times a day should we brush our teeth?

title: Microbes
content: Microbes are tiny living things. We cannot see them without a microscope. Some microbes cause disease. Others are useful, like the bacteria in yoghurt.
question: Can microbes be useful?

title: Food Groups
content: We need different types of food to be healthy. Carbohydrates give us energy. Protein helps us grow. Dairy products help our bones. Fruits give us vitamins.
question: What do carbohydrates give us?

title: Our Solar System
content: The solar system has the sun at its centre. Eight planets orbit the sun. Earth is in the perfect position for life. It has liquid water and the right temperature.
question: What is at the centre of the solar system?

title: Deforestation
content: Deforestation means cutting down forests. This destroys animal habitats. It reduces the oxygen we breathe. It also causes climate change. We must protect our forests.
question: What does deforestation reduce?

title: Joints and Movement
content: Joints are where bones meet. They let us bend and move. There are different kinds of joints. The knee bends one way. The shoulder can rotate in a circle.
question: What are joints?

title: Static Electricity
content: Static electricity is a build-up of charge. Rubbing a balloon on your hair creates static. The balloon then sticks to a wall. Lightning is a huge spark of static electricity.
question: What is lightning?
options: A huge spark of static electricity
answer: A huge spark of static electricity

title: Vitamins and Minerals
content: Vitamins and minerals keep us healthy. Vitamin C in oranges boosts immunity. Calcium in milk makes bones strong. Iron in meat helps our blood carry oxygen.
question: What does Vitamin C do?

title: Animal Classification
content: Scientists sort animals into groups. Mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians and fish are the main groups. This helps scientists study and compare animals.
question: Which group do birds belong to?

title: Habitats Under Threat
content: Many animal habitats are being destroyed. Forests are cut down. Oceans are polluted. Animals lose their homes and food. Some species are becoming extinct.
question: What happens when habitats are destroyed?

title: Buoyancy
content: Buoyancy is the ability to float. Objects float when water pushes them up. This force is called upthrust. It must be greater than the weight of the object.
question: What is the force that pushes floating objects up?

title: Air Resistance
content: Air resistance slows moving objects down. Parachutes use air resistance to fall slowly. A flat shape has more air resistance than a pointed one. Aeroplanes are shaped to reduce it.
question: Why do parachutes fall slowly?

title: Micro-habitats
content: A micro-habitat is a small habitat. Under a log is a micro-habitat. Insects, worms and snails live there. They stay moist and cool under the log.
question: What is a micro-habitat?

title: The Nose
content: Our nose helps us to smell. It filters air as we breathe. Tiny hairs inside trap dust and germs. The sense of smell helps us enjoy food and detect danger.
question: What do tiny hairs in the nose do?

title: Seeds and Fruits
content: A fruit grows from a flower. It protects the seed inside. When the fruit is eaten, the seed can be spread. Apples, tomatoes and mangoes are all fruits.
question: What does a fruit protect?

title: Renewable Energy
content: Wind turbines convert wind into electricity. Solar panels capture sunlight. Hydroelectric dams use flowing water. These are all renewable energy sources.
question: What do solar panels capture?

title: The Ear
content: Our ears help us hear. Sound waves enter the ear. They make the eardrum vibrate. This message goes to the brain. Then we understand the sound.
question: What makes the eardrum vibrate?

title: Acid and Alkali
content: Some liquids are acidic and some are alkaline. Vinegar is an acid. Soap is an alkali. We use indicators to test which one a liquid is. Litmus paper turns red for acid.
question: What does litmus paper turn for an acid?

title: The Intestines
content: After the stomach, food goes to the intestines. Nutrients pass through the walls into the blood. Waste moves through the large intestine and leaves the body.
question: Where do nutrients pass into the blood?

title: Migration
content: Some animals migrate to find food or warm weather. Birds fly south for winter. Whales swim to warm waters. Monarch butterflies travel thousands of miles.
question: Why do birds migrate?
options: To find warm weather and food
answer: To find warm weather and food

title: Solids and Liquids
content: Solids have a fixed shape. Liquids take the shape of their container. If you heat a solid it may melt into a liquid. If you heat a liquid it may turn into a gas.
question: What happens when you heat a solid?

title: Inherited Traits
content: Some features are passed from parents to children. Eye colour and hair colour can be inherited. These features come from genes in our DNA. This is called inheritance.
question: What are inherited features passed through?

title: Biodiversity
content: Biodiversity means having many different species. Healthy ecosystems have high biodiversity. When biodiversity drops, ecosystems become fragile. We should protect all species.
question: What does biodiversity mean?

title: The Tongue
content: Our tongue helps us taste food. There are four main tastes. Sweet, salty, sour and bitter. Taste buds on the tongue detect these flavours. The tongue also helps us speak.
question: What detects flavours on the tongue?

title: Fossil Fuels
content: Fossil fuels are coal, oil and natural gas. They formed from ancient plants and animals over millions of years. Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide and causes climate change.
question: What does burning fossil fuels release?

title: Conservation
content: Conservation means protecting nature. We can plant trees, reduce waste and avoid pollution. Protecting habitats helps endangered animals survive. Everyone can help conserve nature.
question: How can we help endangered animals?
